articles.title: A Tiny QR Code That Can Hold Lots of Information
articles.summary: Scientists have made the world's smallest QR code, which is so tiny it's smaller than bacteria and can only be seen with a special microscope called an electron microscope. This tiny code was created by researchers at TU Wien and a company called Cerabyte, and it's now in the Guinness Book of Records. The QR code is special because it can store data for a very long time, maybe hundreds or thousands of years, unlike regular storage like computer drives that wear out quickly. It's made from a strong material called ceramic, which is used in tools that need to last. The code is engraved into the ceramic using a tiny beam, and each pixel is super small. This means more data can fit in a small space, like fitting over 2 terabytes on a sheet of paper, and it doesn't need any energy to keep the information safe. This could help save our digital knowledge for the future, just like ancient people carved words in stone.

articles.title: Storing Data with Light in 3D
articles.summary: Scientists have found a cool new way to store lots of data using light in three dimensions. They use three parts of light: amplitude, phase, and polarization, which helps fit more information into the same space. This is like writing on a 3D block instead of a flat paper, so it can hold more data and make it faster to save and read. The researchers used a special computer program called a neural network to help read the data from light patterns. This could help make data centers smaller and keep information safer. It's a big step for storing all the data we use every day, like photos and videos.

articles.title: Famous Buildings Go Dark for Earth Hour
articles.summary: On Saturday night, famous buildings like the Eiffel Tower in Paris and Sagrada Família in Barcelona turned off their lights for one hour at 8:30 PM local time. This event is called Earth Hour, and it happens every year to help people think about protecting our planet. It started in Sydney, Australia, in 2007, and is organized by the World Wide Fund for Nature. During Earth Hour, people are encouraged to switch off their lights for 60 minutes to save energy and show they care about the environment. The event brings attention to issues like climate change and encourages everyone to take small actions to help Earth.

articles.title: Why Giant Dragonflies Disappeared: A New Discovery
articles.summary: Long ago, giant dragonflies with wings as wide as a ruler flew in the sky. Scientists thought they needed more oxygen in the air to breathe because insects have tiny tubes for breathing, not lungs like us. As oxygen levels dropped, the big bugs couldn't survive anymore. But a new study shows this idea might be wrong. Researchers looked at many insects, from tiny ones to big beetles, and took pictures of their muscles. They found that the breathing tubes inside the muscles didn't take up much more space in bigger insects. This means the old explanation about oxygen might not be correct, and we still need to learn why giant insects are gone today.

articles.title: Can Events Be in Two Orders at Once? A Quantum Mystery
articles.summary: Scientists are exploring a strange idea in quantum mechanics called "indefinite causal order." This means that in the quantum world, the order of events might not be fixed. For example, imagine two events, A and B. Usually, we think A happens first, then B, or B first, then A. But in quantum experiments, particles like photons can be in a superposition, where they experience both orders at the same time. In a recent experiment, researchers used entangled photons to test this. They sent one photon through a device where it could go through A first or B first, depending on its polarization. The results showed that the photon acted as if it had experienced both orders, suggesting that causality—the idea that causes must come before effects—might not always apply in quantum mechanics. This is like having two timelines mixed together, and it helps scientists understand the weird behavior of tiny particles.

articles.title: The Crab Pulsar's Zebra Stripes Mystery Solved
articles.summary: For over 20 years, scientists were confused by strange stripes in radio waves from the Crab Pulsar, a star leftover from a supernova explosion seen in 1054. In 2024, a scientist from the University of Kansas named Mikhail Medvedev found an answer. He discovered that gravity acts like a lens, bending light in space, which helps create the stripes. The Crab Pulsar is in the Crab Nebula, about 6,500 light-years away, and it sends out radio waves with bright bands and dark gaps, unlike other pulsars. This is the first time scientists have seen gravity and plasma working together to make such a pattern. The findings were shared at a physics meeting and in a science journal, helping us learn more about stars and space.
